content: DINNER SHOW with THE CHRIS CAIN BAND Take some influence from B.B. King, Albert King, Ray Charles, and a pinch of Albert Collins. Add in dazzling blues and jazz guitar chops, a rich soulful baritone vocal delivering original, often wry and beleaguered lyrics with sophisticated chord changes and instrumentation, and skills on various horns and keyboards, all delivered with an uptown cool that never lacks searing passion. It all adds up to the one and only Chris Cain, who has gone from being a newcomer phenomenon bursting onto the blues scene in 1987 with a classic debut release, to being a legend, inspiration and long-established member of the blues pantheon.

content: Dinner & A Show with SLINKY THING- the music of STEELY DAN Slinky Thing is an eight-piece band that has been playing the music of Steely Dan and Donald Fagen to appreciative and enthusiastic ‘Fans of the Dan' for over fifteen years. Band leader Bruce Linde says, “As great as these songs are to listen to, they’re even more fun to play… especially when everyone in the audience is singing along!" With five vocalists and an extensive catalog of the everyone's favorite Steely Dan and Donald Fagen songs, it is no wonder Slinky Thing has been called one of the best Steely Dan tribute bands in the world! Players include: Karla Downey (vocals, percussion), Bruce Linde (vocals, bass), Bill Sparks (sax), Reid Whatley (drums, vocals), Barry Finnerty (vocals, guitar), Brian Osborn (guitar), Neal Roston (keyboards, vocals), and John Pritchard (keyboards). Slinky Thing’s shows typically sell out, so get your tickets now!

title: The Best Community Events in Marin County
description: Our guide to where to find Marin’s best community event. The post The Best Community Events in Marin County appeared first on Marin Magazine .
content: Community is central to many of Marin's cities and towns, and every year the county holds diverse events to salute its people, culture and art. No matter what tickles your fancy or what you like to do on your free weekends, Marin has something special to offer you. The 2025 readers’ choice Best of the County winner is the Marin County Fair (pictured above). For the rest of the Marin Magazine ‘s Best of Marin County Community Event picks see below. Our 2025 Arts & Entertainment category sponsor is MarinArts . View the rest of the Arts & Entertainment category here . Southern Mill Valley – Sausalito Central Fairfax – San Anselmo – San Rafael Northern Novato Southern Marin Mill Valley Fall Arts Festival Mill Valley Photo courtesy of Barry Toranto Once a year, the arts community in Mill Valley gathers amongst the redwoods for a vibrant celebration of creativity. Explore and purchase unique works from over 135 artists, with everything from fine art to handcrafted goods for sale. Plus, enjoy live music, a children’s grove and more. Cascade Way , Mill Valley Sausalito Lighted Boat Parade & Fireworks Sausalito The Sausalito Lighted Boat Parade. Photo by Kimber Communications. The Sausalito Lighted Boat Parade & Fireworks is an unforgettable part of Sausalito’s annual Winterfest. The fleet of boats decked out in shimmering lights floating along the waterfront at nighttime truly has to be seen to be believed. Sausalito Waterfront, Gabrielson Park , Sausalito Central Marin Fairfax Festival Fairfax Fairfax is known for its nonconformist, fun and funky character, making it a draw for any foodie, cycling enthusiast or anyone who wants an escape from the city. Nothing better exemplifies this culture like the Fairfax Festival, a bright flare of food, arts and music. Highlights include the Saturday parade and the kid-zone on the ball field. 66-88 Bolinas Rd , Fairfax Marin County Fair San Rafael Photo courtesy of Marin County Fair Another Readers’ Choice recipient to keep their streak going, they first appeared on the scene in 1925 and embody everything that a nostalgic summer should — pie contests, pig races and live music. Whether it’s your 50th fair experience or your first, you’ll get all the fair feels here. 10 Ave of the Flags , San Rafael, 415.499.6400 San Anselmo Live on the Avenue San Anselmo Photo courtesy of San Anselmo Live on the Avenue Looking for live music? A family-friendly atmosphere? Or perhaps a vibrant community gathering? Live on the Avenue transforms downtown San Anselmo into a bustling pedestrian promenade every Friday and Saturday evening from June through September. With two stages, (Creek Park Amphitheater and Town Hall Plaza) hosting a variety of performances, from blues to indie rock, there’s something for everyone. The event also features rotating activities like movie nights, art exhibitions, and a Kids Zone, making it a perfect outing for all ages. San Anselmo Ave , San Anselmo, 415.258.4640 Northern Marin Novato Art, Wine and Music Festival Novato Photo courtesy of Marin CVB As the name promises, this summer festival has got art, it’s got wine and it’s got music. What more could a person want? Well, one could also want free admission, but this festival has that too! The variety of sponsors, vendors and performers creates a melange of experiences to try out. Grant Ave , Novato, 415.897.1164 Arts & Entertainment Sponsor MarinArts MarinArts is a nonprofit organization that has been proudly promoting the arts and cultural events in Marin County for over ten years. They provide a FREE platform for arts organizations, artists, and venues to share their events through our online calendar. Fun Fact: Their website is accessible in 11 languages — including Spanish, French, and Vietnamese — helping you connect with a wide and diverse audience. MarinArts | San Rafael, CA | 415.578.3856 | marinarts.org Are you a BOC business but want to change your photo, or did we miss including it? Submit your new photo here. The post The Best Community Events in Marin County appeared first on Marin Magazine .

title: Sports Spotlight: Kitty White rewrites the record books
description: For varsity girls basketball senior Kitty White, confidence on the court wasn’t always guaranteed, but it is something she has grown into over the course of her four-year career at Redwood. Basketball wasn’t always her…
content: F or varsity girls basketball senior Kitty White, confidence on the court wasn’t always guaranteed, but it is something she has grown into over the course of her four-year career at Redwood. Basketball wasn’t always her focus; growing up in Australia, she played rugby before moving to the United States in elementary school. After arriving, she decided to try a few new sports and eventually started playing basketball in fourth grade. “I had only ever played rugby in Australia,” White said. “So I just decided to try out some new sports like soccer and basketball, and then I really enjoyed the game.” That rugby background still shows up in the way she plays today. Before discovering her love for basketball, White was a key member on the Marin Highlanders Rugby team. (photo courtesy of Tessa White) “I definitely wasn’t afraid of being physical,” White said. “In rugby you’re going to get tackled and you’re trying to run through people. I think you can kind of see that in my basketball.” Over the years, White’s role on the court has shifted. As a freshman starter, she had the skill but not always the confidence to take over offensively. “I was a very timid player,” White said. “I could score, but I wasn’t always looking for myself to score. I was more focused on setting up my teammates.” Redwood varsity girls basketball head coach Zach Borello highlighted the impact White has had since the start of her high school career. “To start as a freshman is never easy, especially in a big role,” Borello said. “But she handled it flawlessly and continued to grow every year. Her scoring, defen se, rebounding and maturity just kept getting better season after season.” Now, as a senior captain, that mindset has evolved. While White still prioritizes teamwork, she has also grown comfortable stepping up when her team needs it most. “Especially this season, I know my ability to score against anyone,” White said. “If we’re down and we need buckets, I can go get them.” One of White’s biggest strengths is her ability to control the flow of the game and ease the nerves of her teammates. “In really messy games, I think I’m able to calm my teammates down and make sure we’re playing our game. When we get sped up, mistakes happen,” White said. That leadership is something Borello says has defined White’s presence on the team. “She’s a once-in-a-generation player,” Borello said. “She’s the school’s all-time leading scorer and a four-year varsity starter. She uplifts everyone around her and always thinks of others before herself. She’s just a great leader and teammate.” That leadership became especially clear during one of the most memorable moments of her career: Redwood’s North Coast Sections (NCS) semifinal game against Acalanes earlier this season. After losing to the same team earlier in the year, the rematch came down to the wire. “It went to overtime, and I got the game-winner with three seconds left,” White said. “It was definitely a really special game for me.” Borello recalls that game-winning shot as a moment that perfectly captured White’s competitiveness. “We drew up a play for her with eight seconds left, but the play kind of broke down,” Borello said. “She went through three defenders and finished with her left hand for the game-winner. It was a special moment.” White’s mom, Tessa White, reflects on the shot as just one example of the determination she has seen throughout Kitty’s basketball journey. Girls Varsity team holds up their banner celebrating their most successful season in Redwood history. (photo by Joel Kurtzman) “She just wants the team to win,” Tessa White said. “She doesn’t care who scores the points, but when it’s a clutch moment she’ll take it on her back.” That “team-first” mentality has defined White’s career. According to her mother, it is part of what made another milestone especially meaningful this season: becoming Redwood’s all-time leading scorer. “She got a beautiful note from the previous record holder,” Tessa White said. “She said she was proud of Kitty because she’s a team player who scores for the team, not for herself.” Borello expanded on this, mentioning that this mindset has influenced the entire program. “Her leadership and energy set the tone for the team,” Borello said. “When she came with great focus and energy, everyone else followed.” This success, however, did not come without sacrifice. Basketball became a year-round commitment for the White family, with travel, training and club seasons filling much of the calendar. “School holidays were eaten up by travel,” Tessa White said. “But she was lucky because she was doing it with teammates and doing what she loved.” White’s work ethic also stood out to her coaches and teammates. “She was in the gym every morning at 7 a.m. getting shots up,” Borello said. “After practice she would often stay longer to keep shooting. When she was in the gym, others wanted to be there too.” Despite the time commitment, White said her love for the game and her teammates has always been the biggest motivation. “It’s a team sport,” White said. “Even if you’re not feeling great about how you’re playing, you want to be better for your teammates.” Next year, White will continue her basketball career at Cal State Monterey Bay, a decision largely driven by the connection she built with the coaching staff. “The head coach was one of the first college coaches who really showed genuine interest in me,” White said. “When I visited and practiced with the team, I really enjoyed the players and the environment.” That relationship helped White open another opportunity: playing internationally for Great Britain’s youth national team. But for White, the lessons from basketball extend beyond the court. “Hard work pays off,” Tessa White said. “You might not always see it right away, but if you keep showing up and believing, things eventually work out.” For Redwood basketball, those years of hard work have already left their mark. With a record-breaking career and unforgettable moments along the way, White’s legacy on the court is firmly set.

title: March Madness: How to pick a bracket and upsets to look out for
description: Every March, college basketball fans have the opportunity to fill out a free bracket with dreams of taking home a $1 billion prize awarded to the first perfect bracket ever. The odds of this happening are 1 in 9.22 quin…
content: Every March, college basketball fans have the opportunity to fill out a free bracket with dreams of taking home a $1 billion prize awarded to the first perfect bracket ever. The odds of this happening are 1 in 9.22 quintillion. To put that into perspective, you would have a better chance of picking out one single grain of sand on Earth than filling out a perfect bracket, yet millions of brackets are filled out every year only to fail. Red-hot Virginia Cavaliers are a team to look out for when picking this year's bracket. (Photo courtesy of Virginia Sports) Whether you filled out a bracket in your family pool or paid a sum to compete in an expert tournament, it’s important that you didn’t pick teams based on the best logo or funniest mascot. Below, we share our top picks for a winning bracket. For our bracket, we used websites like March Madness Live or the ESPN Tournament Challenge to review a set of metrics for each team before deciding on a matchup. Experts suggest focusing on offensive efficiency when determining first-round success, so we made sure to look at points per game, 3-pointers per game and free throws per game. However, with the transfer portal, name, image and likeness reshaping mid-major rosters, it has become more difficult for No. 13-16 seeds to win games and make deep runs. Bark stuck with the higher seeds early; it is very unlikely that any No. 1 or No. 2 seed loses in the first round, and at most one No. 3 seed will lose. Moving beyond the top seed matchups, when searching for upsets from No. 12-14 seeds, we did not pick teams with a high rank of kill shots against and a low rank of kill shots for. (Kill shots refer to the number of times teams score 10 points without the opposing team scoring a single point. It suggests teams that are streaky and play with a high tempo). Teams like Iowa, Iowa State and Kansas allow a high number of kill shots against them. Another metric that highlights potential vulnerability in the early rounds is a team's tempo ranking. A slower tempo usually means fewer possessions per game and fewer shot attempts. Houston and Virginia both play at a slow pace. Historically, No. 12 seeds beat No. 5 seeds 37 percent of the time, so it's important to have one or two No. 12 seeds advancing to the round of thirty-two. No.13 seeds beat No. 4 seeds 21 percent of the time, so pick at most one No. 13 seed to advance. Round of 64 upsets: East Region: No. 11. South Florida over No. 6. Louisville The University of South Florida (USF) is 14-1 in their last 15 games and are the current American Athletic Conference champions. Louisville, on the other hand, has lost four of its last eight, including losses to Clemson, Miami, Southern Methodist University and the University of North Carolina. Look for USF's strong defense to slow Louisville's fast pace. South Region: No. 12. McNeese over No. 5. Vanderbilt The No. 5 vs. No. 12 matchup in every region could go either way. Vanderbilt has struggled against lackadaisical Southeastern Conference (SEC) play, and McNeese ranks in the top 10 nationally in KenPom offensive efficiency among mid-majors. Look for a high-scoring game with strong performances from McNeese’s guards. West Region: No. 11. Texas/North Carolina State over No. 6. Brigham Young University (BYU) While BYU has shown an ability to play with the best of the BIG 12, in losses, their offense is stagnant, and they tend to take early and mediocre shots due to their inexperience. We predict Texas will take care of the first four matchups and have a field day on the boards and be able to force a grind-it-out game with BYU. No. 10. Missouri over No. 7. Miami (FL) With Miami (FL) coming off a trouncing 84-62 loss to Virginia, and holding only two ranked wins this year, the odds do not appear to be in their favor. To make matters worse for the Miami Canes, this first-round matchup is being played in St. Louis, just a short distance from Missouri’s campus. With many Tigers’ fans expected to be in attendance, the environment is likely to resemble that of a home game and will sway the result in Missouri’s favor. Midwest Region: No. 9. Saint Louis over No. 8. Georgia The Saint Louis Billikens enter the tournament with a roster built for March success, mixing experienced transfers from power conference programs with impressive size. Several rotation players for the Billikens have competed at the highest level before, making the team one of the deepest in the nation. Additionally, most of these rotational players stand at least 6-foot-5, which has led the team to nationally ranked rebounding success throughout the season. If Saint Louis is able to control the boards and use its experience, the 9 seed Billikens could grind out an upset win to kick off the tournament. No. 10. Santa Clara over No. 7. Kentucky After beating Saint Mary’s to play in the West Coast Conference championship, Santa Clara has been riding high on their quick offensive attacks, with four lethal 3-point shooters on the court at all times. We don’t believe in Kentucky's resume and inexperience, and the 3-point volatility from Santa Clara plays in March Madness. Round of 32 upsets: East Region: No. 7. University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) over No. 2. University of Connecticut If leading senior scorer Tyler Bilodeau is labeled healthy for this week, UCLA is a team to watch this March. The team holds top 25 wins against Purdue, Nebraska, Michigan State and Iowa. While they did struggle on the road this season, guard Donovan Dent is playing at an elite level, with recent assist-to-turnover numbers through the roof. The Bruins are peaking at the right time, and their recent consistent play gives them the chance to beat anyone. Battle-tested Wisconsin Badgers have the roster and experience to make a push in this year's tournament. (Photo courtesy of USA Today) West Region: No. 5. Wisconsin over No. 4. Arkansas Wisconsin has undoubtedly had one of the toughest schedules in the country this year, making them battle-tested ahead of the dance. The Badgers have picked up some impressive wins this year over No. 3 Michigan, No. 9 Illinois (2x), No. 10 Michigan St. and No. 15 Purdue, showing they can compete with the best. Arkansas being the SEC champions means much less this year as the conference holds one top 10 team (Florida), who just lost by 17 points to SEC runner-up Vanderbilt. Wisconsin's quality experience in high-level matchups should be enough to give them an edge in a highly competitive second-round matchup. Sweet 16 upsets: No. 7. UCLA over No. 3. Michigan State UCLA recently proved they can beat Michigan State, knocking off the Spartans 88-84 in the Big Ten Tournament quarterfinals. UCLA guard Donovan Dent led the charge with 23 points and 12 assists while the Bruins shot 55 percent from the field, knocking down 13 3-pointers. Michigan State attempted a late comeback, outscoring the Bruins 51-44 in the second half, but due to their poor first-half performance, the Spartans fell just short. If UCLA’s guards continue to control the game and create scoring opportunities as they did in that matchup, then there is no reason why the Bruins aren’t set up for yet another upset. South: No. 4. Nebraska over No. 1. Florida Our first No. 1 seed to vacate, Florida, was recently smacked by Vanderbilt (91-74) in the SEC championship and has lost games to Auburn, Missouri, UConn, Texas Christian University and Arizona. Nebraska, on the other hand, has faced the gauntlet in the BIG 10 and is getting healthy at the right time. Look for a low-scoring mismatch and the Cornhuskers to slow down the pace and limit Florida's kill shots. Midwest: No. 3. Virginia over No. 2. Iowa State The 29-5 Virginia Cavaliers rank near the top 25 nationally in both offensive and defensive efficiency, often doing a good job at limiting opponents’ three-point success, forcing the Duke Blue Devils to shoot 32 percent from three in their last matchup. The Cavaliers also rely on depth with nine different players averaging at least 16 minutes per game, helping them stay fresh towards the end of tight matchups. If Virginia can continue their strong defense, the high-scoring Cyclones’ offense will be forced into tougher shots and poor possessions that will see the Cavaliers punch their ticket to the Elite Eight. Our Final Four: Duke vs Illinois Purdue vs Michigan Our Championship and Winner: Duke over Michigan 74-63

title: The High Green Chilly Winds
description: The High Green Chilly Winds manage to channel that elusive Dead vibe uniquely, with wide-open guitar tones, effortless three-part vocal harmonies, choogling beats, and yes, plenty of tripped out, shredded solos. The loo…
content: The High Green Chilly Winds manage to channel that elusive Dead vibe uniquely, with wide-open guitar tones, effortless three-part vocal harmonies, choogling beats, and yes, plenty of tripped out, shredded solos. The look, the sound, the atmosphere is uncanny. Far from being a historical re-enactment, this band's laissez faire flow infuses the band with a gentle spirit, warmth, and (dare we say it) authenticity. From their killer merch game to their eminently watchable YouTube channel, they're clearly having a real good time spreading the love. Strangely enough, in a world overflowing with wax museum nostalgia and Deadly sentimentalism, we need the Chilly Winds, now more than ever.

title: Moonalice Sunday Matinee
description: Moonalice is an exuberant Bay Area ten-piece known for delivering a unique brand of psychedelic soul and rock-tinged Americana. With an unparalleled list of collaborations with some of the biggest names in music, the fa…
content: Moonalice is an exuberant Bay Area ten-piece known for delivering a unique brand of psychedelic soul and rock-tinged Americana. With an unparalleled list of collaborations with some of the biggest names in music, the family tree of Moonalice has touchedevery corner of rock and roll history. Led by vocalist Lester Chambers, who co-founded pioneering '60s psychedelic soul group The Chambers Brothers; bassist Pete Sears, a founding member of Jefferson Starship who has played with everyone from Rod Stewart to Jimi Hendrix, and guitarist Roger McNamee, who was an advisor to Grateful Dead and U2 and fights against entrenched power in the tech industry - their incredible chemistry shines through in their live performances and the recent release of the Chambers Brothers classic, "Time Has Come Today." Moonalice also features esteemed members Barry Sless (lead guitar and pedal steel), Jason Crosby (keyboards), Grammy winner John Molo (drums), along with the next generation of legends including Lester's son Dylan Chambers, and Erika, Rachel, and Chloe Tietjen of acclaimed Americana band the T Sisters. Moonalice has a renegade spirit and an ethos of love, peace, and happiness that permeates everything they do.

title: Dark N Stormy
description: Dark N Stormy has been delivering sets of original renditions of timeless jam-based and funky classics to packed crowds in the San Francisco Bay Area since their formation in 2015. Guitarists Jeff Webb and Pete Petitt l…
content: Dark N Stormy has been delivering sets of original renditions of timeless jam-based and funky classics to packed crowds in the San Francisco Bay Area since their formation in 2015. Guitarists Jeff Webb and Pete Petitt lead the way with intertwining and alternating string work, complemented by the driving keys of John Amster. Each trades off with lead vocals, and three-part harmonies rise from the mix. All is stacked on a rock-solid rhythm section coupling Kevin Woodhouse on drums with Scott Lipsitz on bass. We play our own revival of jam-based and funky classics, rooted in the nostalgic sounds the Grateful Dead, the Allman Brothers Band, Little Feat and other giants of jam. Our music is familiar and never the same twice! Visit us at www.darknstormy.band.

title: Teddy Thompson
description: Beloved London-born, Brooklyn-based singer/songwriter Teddy Thompson has returned with the exquisitely crafted Never Be The Same, his first collection of original material since 2020. Across ten tracks, Thompson refines…
content: Beloved London-born, Brooklyn-based singer/songwriter Teddy Thompson has returned with the exquisitely crafted Never Be The Same, his first collection of original material since 2020. Across ten tracks, Thompson refines his craft via an exploration of music's enduring preoccupations — love, longing, and the uneasy passage of time. This album wasn't built upon a grand narrative. There was no self-imposed exile, no forced reinvention. Instead, it is centered around an exhortation that is threaded through the songs like a refrain: "Never Be The Same," its title only revealing itself to Thompson after he'd completed the recording. "It's a phrase that, unconsciously, I used twice. And when I saw it on the page, I realized, this is the message of this album," says Thompson. "Don't ever be the same. Change. Grow! Even when the sentiment is, woe is me, I'll never recover after that love or loss. The message is still, change. Don't get too comfortable. Everything is temporary, so evolve or perish!" For Thompson, the search for this truth starts with authenticity and personal experience. "Songwriting is magical. You can hear one hundred people sing ‘I love you,' and you know which one is telling the truth," Thompson says. "If the root of the sentiment is authentic, it will resonate."

title: San Miguel Fraser
description: San Miguel Fraser is redefining folk music with their dynamic fusion of Castilian and Celtic traditions. The duo, featuring María San Miguel—an Oviedo Conservatory graduate deeply rooted in the vibrant music of Castile—…
content: San Miguel Fraser is redefining folk music with their dynamic fusion of Castilian and Celtic traditions. The duo, featuring María San Miguel—an Oviedo Conservatory graduate deeply rooted in the vibrant music of Castile—and Galen Fraser—a Berklee College of Music alumnus and son of the legendary fiddler Alasdair Fraser, blends heartfelt vocals, intricate string arrangements, and the lively charm of the cittern. Their music celebrates the shared spirit of their homelands while embracing the beauty of innovation. Their latest album, Dots of Light, is a testament to their artistry, shining with vibrant new compositions and imaginative reworkings of traditional tunes. With their unique chamber-folk style, San Miguel Fraser crafts performances that resonate deeply with audiences worldwide. The band has captivated listeners at prestigious festivals and venues, including Celtic Connections, Festival Internacional de Ortigueira, Sisters Folk Fest, KVMR Celtic Festival, and Miguel Delibes Auditorium. Whether performing at intimate gatherings or grand stages, they bring a heartfelt connection and a celebratory spirit to every performance.

title: Duane Betts & Palmetto Motel with Miko Marks
description: Duane Betts ignites and brings an evolution to the Southern rock tradition. With a fiery legacy forged by his father, the legendary Dickey Betts, the second generation musician infuses it with his own soul-stirring blen…
content: Duane Betts ignites and brings an evolution to the Southern rock tradition. With a fiery legacy forged by his father, the legendary Dickey Betts, the second generation musician infuses it with his own soul-stirring blend of blues, raw passion, and six-string mastery. Backed by his band, Palmetto Motel, Betts delivers a dynamic live show filled with searing guitar solos, heartfelt storytelling, and a mix of classic influences with fresh energy. Fans can expect a blend of roots rock, blues, and some improvisational jamming, creating an electrifying yet deeply authentic experience. With top-tier musicianship, a connection to rock ‘n' roll history, and the desire to make every performance a true experience, a Palmetto Motel show is an unforgettable journey that no music lover should miss. Palmetto Motel is… Johnny Stachela (guitar, vocals) Pedro Arevalo (bass, vocals) Vincent Fossett Jr. (drums) Max Butler (B-3 organ) After a 14-year hiatus, Miko Marks has spent the last few years making up for lost time. Her early career as a country singer brought critical acclaim but also a series of industry execs who didn't think they could market her to their audiences. In 2021 she re-emerged as a "monster talent" according to Rolling Stone, drawing on the full scope of her musical and cultural roots, and has quickly become a fan favorite at festivals and venues all over the country. With a vintage sound that deftly blends Americana, blues, southern rock, and even gospel, her powerful voice and warm, soulful spirit literally bring every audience to its feet. She follows up Our Country (2021), Race Records (2021 EP), Feel Like Going Home (2022) and its 2024 deluxe version (feat. Buddy Miller, Tré Burt, Melissa Carper...) with a blues album coming in 2026.

title: Game day or rain delay? Spring sports face unpredictable weather conditions
description: Unpredictable and extreme weather conditions are increasingly affecting outdoor sports, leading to concerns with the spring sports season looming ahead. From heavy rain to strong winds and low temperatures, shifting wea…
content: Unpredictable and extreme weather conditions are increasingly affecting outdoor sports, leading to concerns with the spring sports season looming ahead. From heavy rain to strong winds and low temperatures, shifting weather conditions have disrupted outdoor sports and created challenges for both athletes and coaches. The number of practices canceled and races postponed has increased year by year, with sports such as softball and track being particularly vulnerable to these changing conditions. Canceled competitions and shortened practices can limit preparation time, impact team momentum and create scheduling conflicts for teams trying to reschedule events within an already short season. Junior softball player Aria Sreba said that it was rare for sessions to get postponed her freshman year, whereas this year, multiple practices and a game have already been postponed. “We've had several practices canceled due to the rain,” Sreba said. “Field conditions-wise, it's not good to practice on. Especially with the muddy fields, as they can get flooded.” Construction on the new music buildings has resulted in only one softball field for both varsity and junior varsity (JV) to use, making it harder to reschedule games after weather cancellations. Due to construction this year, softball only has one field between varsity and junior varsity (JV), which makes scheduling practices especially difficult. “We only have one field this season, and it's definitely hard for both varsity and JV softball,” Sreba said. “Both teams get impacted by [the weather].” The softball field is located next to the Corte Madera Creek, which experiences significant water overflow during heavy rain and flooding, meaning the field has a limited time frame for use. “From the beginning of the season, even during tryouts, we were getting rained out. We’d have to practice in the gym, which consists mostly of hitting.” Sreba said. “While it can be beneficial, it's not the best for practices overall, because you want to be out on the field to get your practice in.” The concerning part is that the cancellation of sporting events is increasing exponentially, Sreba said, largely due to both the rain and high tides that happened both in January and February. “I couldn’t remember any games being rained out my freshman year or even sophomore year. I don't remember games getting rained out, but I'm definitely seeing it Infographic by Eleanor Bentley more this year,” Sreba said. “[We had] our first game of the season and we had to push it back [because of the rain].” Junior Devon Stoll-Morrill competes in pole vault and high jump as part of the track and field team and finds that while the weather is noticeable, it doesn’t affect the track season all that much. “We've only had a single practice canceled due to the storms that occurred over winter break,” Stoll-Morrill said. She also added that in her three years on the team, they’ve rarely had practices or meets canceled due to weather. However, Stoll-Morrill notices the added challenges extreme weather presents to athletes. “Rain and wind can make it more difficult to do sports because they can both get in your way. If it's raining, it can make it difficult to see or coordinate if you’re approaching [your jump]. And if you’re going up in the air and it’s windy, you can get blown off course,” Stoll-Morrill said. “Especially with pole vaulting, [since] you go a lot higher up, the wind can affect you more.” As the spring season continues, teams will continue to adjust their schedules due to the changing weather conditions. For many outdoor sports, weather has become an additional factor shaping practices, competitions and preparation.

title: Girls and boys varsity swim sends seniors off by celebrating senior night with a win against San Rafael
description: On Friday Mar. 20, girls and boys swim celebrated and honored the seniors on the team with a senior night celebration followed by a dominant win against San Rafael. The girls won 105-36 and the boys won 122-18. Seniors…
content: Gallery • 4 Photos Continuing the tradition of teammates making posters for individual seniors, Vendula Davidova, Morgan Sicklick, Gabriella Grow, Rebecca Duran, Shane Pastel and Oliver Rogers (left to right) gather for a group picture holding their handmade signs. On Friday Mar. 20, girls and boys swim celebrated and honored the seniors on the team with a senior night celebration followed by a dominant win against San Rafael. The girls won 105-36 and the boys won 122-18. Seniors Vendula Davidova, Morgan Sicklick, Shane Pastel, Rebecca Duran, Oliver Rogers and Gabriella Grow were all honored by their team, family and coaches. Handmade signs and flowers were presented to them by their coaches and teammates. When looking back at the time spent in the pool over the years, Pastel felt that swimming was a constant place to find peace. “Swimming has just become such a great way for me to escape my stress cause I feel like swimming is very therapeutic and no matter where I am I know I can always swim and reconnect with it,” Pastel said. Davidova, a new addition to the swim team, expressed how swimming has impacted her Redwood experience and has made her feel welcome. “I love practices and the community. I feel much more welcome at school. The community is a really big part. The girls, guys and coaches are all amazing,” Davidova said. The impact the seniors have on the team continues outside of the pool by providing a mentor to younger athletes on the team. Sophomore Aviva Berkowitz spoke about how the seniors have been supportive teammates and good role models. “They encourage us to try new events and help push past any mental blocks through sharing their own experiences and making sure you don’t feel alone with your experience on the team,” Berkowitz said. As they prepare for their departure next season, the seniors left some advice for younger swimmers. “Don’t be scared, just jump into the water and just swim. Even though you might be nervous, it’s gonna go away the second you’re in the water,” Davidova said.

title: 3 Local Marin Businesses That Prioritize Sustainability
description: At the Helix Mastermind 2026 Winter Conference, CEOs of local businesses shared their success stories and how they prioritize sustainability. The post 3 Local Marin Businesses That Prioritize Sustainability appeared fir…
content: With more than 4,000 brands in the California Green Business Network , Marin proudly boasts more than 430 certified green businesses in the county, according to the San Rafael Chamber of Commerce . Last month, at a Helix Mastermind conference at Cavallo Point , Marin Magazine had the honor of talking to three local entrepreneurs who care as much about the planet (and people) as making a profit — and they’ve succeeded in both. Here’s how Helen Russell of Equator Coffees , Susan Griffin-Black of EO and Everyone for everybody , and Mike Higgins of Passport Resorts ( Cavallo Point and Post Ranch Inn ) were able to maintain their passion for the planet as they built their businesses. Equator Coffees Co-founded in 1995 by Helen Russell in a Marin County garage The first California coffee roaster to become a Certified B Corporation, Equator blends social and environmental impact with business. The brand uses Loring Smart Roasters , which, according to Loring, claims to cut natural gas usage and carbon emissions by about 80%, compared to traditional roasting. Equator also sources coffee from regenerative and Fair Trade partners worldwide, supporting ecosystem health and fair pricing for farming communities. Read our interview with Helen Russell here . EO and Everyone for everybody Founded in 1995 by Susan Griffin-Black and Brad Black in San Francisco (also in their garage) Today, the San Rafael-based company manufactures and distributes its EO and Everyone products — everything from shampoo to hand sanitizer and more — nationwide through major retailers like Whole Foods and Target, as well as online and select international markets. EO Products manufactures in a zero-waste facility, diverting over 90% of waste from landfills. Their bottles are BPA-free with high recycled content , and packaging is 100% recyclable. They partner globally with ethical suppliers for quality essential oils and botanical extracts while maintaining strict sustainability standards. Read our interview with Susan Griffin-Black here. Passport Resorts Founded in 1990 in a living room in Sausalito by Michael Freed and Peter Heinemann A pioneer in sustainable luxury hotel management, Passport Resorts’ property portfolio includes the likes of Cavallo Point Lodge and Post Ranch Inn. Cavallo Point Lodge is LEED Gold certified, adaptively reuses historic buildings and sources about 90% of its food locally. It also supports environmental nonprofits through its eponymous Cavallo Point Fund. Meanwhile, Post Ranch Inn’s 990-panel solar array offsets roughly 600,000 pounds of carbon annually, while preserving 90% of its land to protect and support endangered species. Both properties use energy-efficient systems, prioritize native landscaping to reduce irrigation, and protect local ecosystems and maintain robust recycling and composting programs to minimize landfill waste. Read our interview with Mike Higgins here. The post 3 Local Marin Businesses That Prioritize Sustainability appeared first on Marin Magazine .

title: “Dirty Jobs” Host Mike Rowe on the Rise of Trade Jobs, and AI’s Surprising Role
description: TV host and Marin resident Mike Rowe talks about the rise of trade jobs. The post "Dirty Jobs" Host Mike Rowe on the Rise of Trade Jobs, and AI’s Surprising Role appeared first on Marin Magazine .
content: Reports of a tough job market may be greatly exaggerated, at least depending on whom you ask. Yes, Americans with a bachelor’s degree account for a quarter of the unemployed . And professional occupations susceptible to AI automation have seen sharp spikes in joblessness. But trade work, the labor that is critical to build and maintain critical infrastructure of everyday life, is having quite a moment. For the first time ever, high-school graduates are finding jobs quicker than college graduates , an unprecedented trend. According to the federal Bureau of Labor Statistics , skilled trade jobs are projected to see 9% growth from 2024 to 2034, adding roughly 81,000 positions annually. Data like this is music to the ears of Marin resident Mike Rowe. The affable Rowe made a name for himself as host of the Discovery Channel’s Dirty Jobs . In 2008, he launched the Mike Rowe Works Foundation , a nonprofit that aims to help close the skills gap by challenging the stigmas and stereotypes that discourage people from pursuing trade work. Since then, the organization has doled out more than $16 million in scholarships. Trade jobs are especially alive and well in Marin County, where November 2025 data from the state Employment Development Department indicated that about 28,000 of 112,800 non-farm jobs in the county—roughly 25% — were trade jobs. We recently caught up with Rowe to get a sense of the state of industry work and what he thinks the future holds for trade jobs across the region. How would you describe the state of industry work among young people today? I’d say that the skilled trades, in general, are on the verge of a renaissance, and that young people, in general, will be the primary beneficiaries. The renaissance, however, is not happening because of a newfound appreciation or respect for the work. The stigmas, stereotypes, myths and misperceptions that surround the skilled trades are still real and still in need of debunking. But the cold, hard calculus of supply and demand is inviting people to look closer at many vocational opportunities, and those opportunities are very attractive right now. What is that calculus, exactly? It’s more like simple math, really. On the supply side, we’ve got a demographic challenge. For every five tradespeople who retire, two replace them. It’s been that way for over a decade, and that ratio is obviously not sustainable. Combine our shrinking workforce with a longstanding image problem around the skilled trades, and it’s easy to see why the labor shortage has become so acute. Meanwhile, on the demand side, you have the sudden realization that the future of the entire AI economy is completely reliant on a skilled workforce. We’re now on the verge of what many are calling the “greatest infrastructure project of all time,” and the need for skilled tradespeople is about to become dire. Good news for those entering the industry. But bad news for an industry that’s going to have to fight for skilled workers and a country that needs to compete with China. How do you think people perceive trade jobs in this current economy? I think a lot of parents and guidance counselors are still clinging (stubbornly) to the idea that a four-year degree is the “best path for the most people” (especially their kids) and are still skeptical that a career in the trades offers a truly viable alternative. But anecdotally, after 17 years of running a trade school scholarship program, I can assure you that perceptions are changing with Gen Z. This year, we received 10x the number of applications we received the year before and awarded $5 million in scholarships. We could have doubled that had we known the interest would be so high. There's all this talk about AI eliminating the need for human workers. What impact will AI have on trade jobs? In the near term, I think AI is going to fuel exponential demand in the trades, in much the same way the Internet fueled demand in coding and software design. Thousands of data centers are going to be built, like it or not. The energy industry, the construction industry, the shipbuilding industry and the automotive industry will be hiring skilled workers. If you could retrain as a tradesperson today, what three trades would you consider and why? At the moment, electricians, plumbers and welders are all in demand. Last month, at a data center in Plano, Texas, I met three electricians who had as much overtime as they wanted. All were making north of $250,000 a year. All were under 30. None had any debt, and all of them had been poached multiple times for similar projects. I hear the same stories from the 3,300 people my own foundation has helped train. The benefits of mastering a skill that’s in demand are now undeniable, and those skills are being taught in trade schools. If someone wants to get into trade work, how and where should they start? I’d start by [searching online] to list the best trade schools in my area for whatever trade I wanted to explore. Then, I’d apply for a work ethic scholarship from the mikeroweWORKS Foundation. I’d also [search online] to list all the similar scholarship programs out there and apply to those as well. There’s a lot of scholarship money out there, and no reason to start a career in the trades with a pile of debt. Sausalito Program Spotlights Marine Trades Most trade jobs in Marin County focus on work done on land—construction, plumbing, HVAC installation and more. One class of jobs focuses on skills applied in a very different setting: the maritime industry. Today, through a program at the Spaudling Marine Center in Sausalito, a select number of local workers get to apprentice as boat service technicians. The program, Boatworks 101, is a paid, 12-month internship that introduces young adults to the marine trades through hands-on training and an American Boat & Yacht Council-registered curriculum. The program provides broad exposure to boat maintenance and repair skills, followed by an industry placement, preparing graduates for entry-level careers in the marine industry So far the program has graduated 26 boat service technicians. These individuals have gone on to pursue careers in diesel engine service, shipwright work, marine electrical systems, outboard mechanics, varnishing, rigging, and other marine trades. According to Executive Director Matt Zarem, the program solves a dual-sided issue. “Interested people are eager to learn a new set of skills, and boatyards are having a tough time finding and hiring qualified people to do the work that boat-owners are looking to have done on their boats,” he said. “Through this program, we’re trying to address both sides of the equation.” The program was established in 2021, and launched its fifth cohort in August 2025. Typically, apprentices spend the first nine months of the program identifying and learning about a specialization. Half the time apprentices are learning theory, the other half they’re working in the yard with a mentor. Once this portion of the program concludes, participants spend the final three months in an internship with a local business. Often, this work experience leads to guaranteed employment upon graduation. In five years, leaders said the program has had real impact on the local maritime business. In particular, Program Director Kelly Gregory said Boatworks 101 has graduated several riggers (tradespeople with expertise in ropes and pulleys), finishers (tradespeople who work with fiberglass), and machinists (tradespeople who fabricate and fix propellers, pumps, and more). “Just like drivers can’t live without their auto mechanics, boaters can’t live without their boat service technicians,” said Program Director Kelly Gregory. “If there aren’t folks to work on boats, what happens when they need fixing?” The post "Dirty Jobs" Host Mike Rowe on the Rise of Trade Jobs, and AI’s Surprising Role appeared first on Marin Magazine .

title: The Rock and Roll Playhouse plays Music of Bob Marley + More for Kids
description: Children under 1 are free. The Rock and Roll Playhouse, a family concert series hosted at historic music venues across the country, allows kids to "move, play and sing while listening to works from the classic-rock cano…
content: Children under 1 are free. The Rock and Roll Playhouse, a family concert series hosted at historic music venues across the country, allows kids to "move, play and sing while listening to works from the classic-rock canon" (NY Times). Performing songs created by the most iconic musicians in rock history, The Rock and Roll Playhouse band offers its core audience of families with children age ten and under games, movement, stories and an opportunity to rock out in an effort to educate children and explore their creativity. The Rock and Roll Playhouse is an early and often first introduction to a child's lifelong journey with live music and rock and roll. See you at the show!This concert for kids and families shares the music of Grateful Dead with a new generation of music lovers, but is not associated with or endorsed by the artist. All children must be accompanied by a parent or adult caregiver. The Rock and Roll Playhouse knows that little ears are sensitive, so we turn down the volume at our shows. However, all kids experience music differently and you may find that your child is more comfortable wearing hearing protection earmuffs.Please note: By attending this event, you consent to your image (with or without your voice) being included in photographs and/or film and videotape of the event, and give the venue, The Rock and Roll Playhouse and their respective licensees and assigns the absolute and irrevocable right and permission to use and publish such content in any and all media, whether now known or hereafter developed. Sweetwater Music Hall Inc. is a non-profit 501(c)(3) organization that is fully devoted to advancing music education, understanding and appreciation and Bay Area music history, culture and arts.

title: How Four Marin Leaders See the Future of Workforce Housing in Marin
description: As part of our ongoing look at the housing crisis in the county, we spoke with four Marin leaders about the issue and some ideas they think could help. The post How Four Marin Leaders See the Future of Workforce Housing…
content: Twenty-five hundred years ago, the Greek philosopher Heraclitus articulated a fundamental law of the Universe when he proclaimed that life is a constant state of flux. Here in Marin County, we have tested that principle for decades, as Marinites have strived to preserve life as we have always known it in the county, including idyllic vistas, emerald open spaces and low-density housing. While the vistas and open spaces are not going anywhere, local leaders say Marin’s resistance to affordable housing is no longer sustainable. As land and housing prices continue to soar, they believe that workforce housing projects are critical for the environment and our quality of life. “Everyone wants Marin to stay the same,” says Sarah Jones, Marin County Director of Community Development. “The paradox is that Marin cannot stay the same unless we have workforce housing for people who work here.” The “workforce” Jones refers to includes teachers, law enforcement officers, healthcare workers, caregivers for the elderly and other essential workers who either will not take jobs in Marin because they cannot afford to live here, or must commute from afar, impacting their ability to do their jobs, increasing home-insecurity and adding cars (and pollution) to our already congested roadways. These same local leaders also believe that we have entered the moment for change — a historical juncture where not only is the California state government imposing mandates on the county, but the majority of Marin citizens recognize that we must provide housing for the people who ensure that our communities thrive. From politicians to government employees, developers to nonprofit leaders, a multitude of voices are describing a sustainable future for the County, and calling for more rapid progress toward that future. Jenny Silva, Executive Director of Call Marin Home Call Marin Home (formerly the Marin Environmental Housing Collaborative or MEHC) is a Marin County nonprofit coalition that advocates for affordable and sustainable housing. The mission, according to Executive Director Jenny Silva, is to “expand housing through production, preservation, and protections for an inclusive Marin County.” Recognizing the complexity of the housing landscape, Call Marin Home both advocates for new housing projects and coordinates local nonprofits, developers, legal advocates and community groups to address the housing crisis in the county. Collaborators include Habitat for Humanity, Homeward Bound, Legal Aid of Marin, Canal Alliance, Thompson-Dorfman/Educational Housing Partners, Eden Housing and others. “Older people can’t hire caretakers, people are recognizing there is not a pathway for their children to move back to Marin, we have teacher turnover and shortages because teachers cannot live here in Marin. Honestly, it’s embarrassing,” says Silva. In a 2025 opinion piece for the Marin Independent Journal in support of the Oak Hill Apartments project in Larkspur near San Quentin, Silva points out that a 2022 report from Marin Promise Partnership found that 43% of Marin school staffers “could not afford a studio apartment anywhere in Marin.” She also notes that, according to the Novato Unified School District, “teachers between 25 and 34 years old stay on the job for an average of only 1.6 years.” (According to the article, turnover has “increased from 3% to 14% over an 11-year period,” while Marin officials estimate that it can cost “as much as $25,000 per year to recruit a replacement.”) “We cannot make the change that is needed by adding another 40 units here or there. In some towns, there’s just one project with people fighting it tooth and nail,” says Silva. “People don’t realize what an outlier Marin County is. They think a one year delay on a project is acceptable. Elsewhere, a one year delay would be considered horrible.” The California state mandated Regional Housing Needs Allocation (RHNA) for Marin County is 14,210 units by 2031, and, according to Silva, the progress has been extremely slow due to lawsuits filed by anti-growth groups that delay construction. “People say, we’ve gone too far, we’ll have runaway development,” she says. “There are 330 units, total, under development at the moment. We’re not even close to what we need to do.” At the same time, Silva adds, she has noticed change on the ground amongst the people of Marin in recent years. “It used to be that when we stood up to advocate for a project, we stood up alone. Now there are others, sometimes people who are not even affiliated with any organization.” Sarah Jones, Director of Community Development for Marin County Photo by Jim Jones Marin County Director of Community Development Sarah Jones agrees with Silva that there has been a shift in sentiment around housing. “From the outside it looks like we’re not getting too far,” she says. “But compared to five or 10, even three years ago, we’re in a better place. Something like the Marinwood Apartments project, a mixed-use infill development of 125 affordable units that has been approved, in the past, we could not get a project like that going.” Marinwood Apartments — located at the former Marinwood Plaza Shopping Center site just off the freeway — will offer one-, two- and three‑bedroom residences for households at 30–70% of Area Median Income (AMI). The project, funded by Marinwood Propco, L.P. and Tableau Development, faced a lawsuit that was voluntarily dismissed last summer, while the County has committed Affordable Housing Trust Fund loans to help fill financing gaps so the development can move forward. “I would love to see more projects like Marinwood Apartments,” says Jones. “Marinwood is what we call ‘gentle density,’ meaning it is development at a scale that makes sense in the community.” As a former planning director for the San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency, Jones is an advocate for access to regional transit as a critical piece to making workforce housing development sustainable. “I would like to see a stronger connection between all the pieces (including transit) that it takes to make housing work.” Another recent improvement in the housing landscape, says Jones, is the ongoing development of the Community Land Trust Network of Marin , which connects the various land trusts across Marin County. “The County is supporting this network and helping it to coalesce to provide a scaffolding and the sharing of resources for the land trusts across Marin,” says Jones. Land trusts are community based nonprofits that purchase land or property, and take it off the open market, generally for conservation or to stabilize housing costs for local workers. In 2019, Marin County purchased 54 units of former Coast Guard housing in Point Reyes, where the lack of workforce housing is a longstanding problem and has prevented sustainable agriculture from truly being sustainable. The County is working with the Community Land Trust Association of West Marin (CLAM) to ensure the homes, priced for low-income families and agricultural workers, will remain affordable for the long run. Homeowners will be able to build equity to benefit from homeownership, but when they sell, the price is limited by a formula that keeps the home affordable for the next buyer. For rental housing, the rate is set based on income levels rather than market rates. Andrea Osgood, Chief of Real Estate Development, Eden Housing One of the most impactful in-progress workforce housing projects in Marin is the Oak Hill Apartments project near San Quentin in unincorporated Larkspur. Oak Hill will sit on 8.5 acres of surplus state land between San Quentin Village and the Larkspur Landing waterfront, and will create 250 new affordable rental homes, split between two residential communities: one developed by Eden Housing, a Bay Area housing nonprofit, and one by Education Housing Partners (EHP), the nonprofit branch of Mill Valley based Thompson-Dorfman developers. The community developed by Eden Housing will comprise 115 units for low-income households, with construction currently slated to begin this year, and residents moving in in 2028. “Eden was founded by a small group of retirees who became fair housing activists back in 1968. They held their first meetings at an I-Hop,” says Andrea Osgood, Eden’s Head of Real Estate Development. Over the years, Eden Housing has purchased over 12,000 units of affordable housing across California, and following Governor Newsom’s 2019 call to action to search statewide for unused land for development, Eden was chosen to build the Oak Hill project on unused land in Larkspur. “We lean into our work in Marin County,” says Osgood. “And we are excited to partner with the State and commend the Governor. At a time when it is hard to find actual dollars, the Governor asked, ‘What are the resources we already have to make a project possible?'" Meanwhile, the community developed by EHP will consist of 135 units dedicated to low- to moderate-income educators, school support staff and County of Marin employees. Introducing an innovative financing method, EHP has partnered with the County and the Marin County Office of Education to create a Joint Powers Authority to own and finance the workforce housing component. This will allow districts to offer their teachers and staff below-market rate housing in Marin, helping teachers to stay in Marin districts. Eric Lucan, Marin County Supervisor For Marin County elected officials, the approach to housing has been a tricky push and pull, balancing the need to comply with California laws while also addressing local environmental concerns, zoning restrictions and infrastructure and financial shortfalls. Marin County Supervisor Eric Lucan, currently the President of the Board for 2026, is one of the most visible champions of housing projects on the Board of Supervisors, and has been a strong advocate for permanent supportive housing for the homeless and other vulnerable populations. In 2023, as the State put pressure on counties, he helped pass the Marin County Housing Element, a plan outlining Marin’s approach to providing supportive and workforce housing. Now, as President, he has placed housing solutions at the top of the list for the Board of Supervisor’s 2026 agenda. “There is no question there is significant demand for workforce housing in the County,” says Lucan “Surveys come back, again and again and again, that show that workforce affordable housing is a top priority — and that is across the board, including Marin homeowners.” He points out that over the past 40 years home prices in Marin have increased by over 800%, while inflation-adjusted income has only gone up 40–60%. From Lucan’s perspective, the space, leadership and collective’s will to build exists in the County, but the primary obstacle is financing. “We have many very good projects that are shovel-ready, such as the Habitat Redwood Boulevard project in Novato, but the high cost of construction, labor and materials, and where interest rates are right now, makes it hard for developers to make these projects pencil out.” The Habitat for Humanity Redwood Boulevard development will provide 80 homes for low and moderate income households. There are numerous funding sources, including large county loans, city grants, state programs and various subsidies and fee waivers, but Habitat still faces a shortfall for the $85 million dollar project. Lucan is currently running for California’s State Assembly, and says, if elected, he will work on this piece of the puzzle from Sacramento. “There’s a big lens in Sacramento on streamlining affordable housing right now. The next question is, how do we unlock more financing?” Community Land Trusts in Marin Community Land Trust Association of West Marin (CLAM) Bolinas Community Land Trust The Mount Tam Community Land Trust Two Valleys Community Land Trust Hope Housing in Marin City Kirsten Jones Neff is a journalist who writes about all things North Bay, with special attention to the environment and the region’s farmers, winemakers and food artisans. She also works and teaches in school gardens. Kirsten’s poetry collection, When The House Is Quiet , was nominated for the Northern California Book Award , and three of her poems received a Pushcart nomination . She lives in Novato with her husband and three children and tries to spend as much time as possible on our local mountains, beaches and waterways. For more on her work visit KirstenJonesNeff.Com. The post How Four Marin Leaders See the Future of Workforce Housing in Marin appeared first on Marin Magazine .

title: A Place for Everything: Your Home Renovation Disposal Guide
description: Executive Director of the Good Future Design Alliance, Kathryn Soter, offers her top tips for responsibly disposing of home renovation waste. The post A Place for Everything: Your Home Renovation Disposal Guide appeared…
content: If you’re planning a home renovation in Marin County, you’re likely focused on choosing the right countertops, finding a reliable contractor and staying on budget. But there’s another consideration that deserves a spot on your checklist: what happens to everything you’re removing? When I first moved into my home back in the ’90s — a pre-war, summer cottage — I had a choice: gut it or live with some interesting design features, like pink and burgundy tile, a knotty pine kitchen and systems that were designed for another century. While some of these touches were charming, others were genuinely unsafe. But with both financial and time constraints — a baby in tow and one on the way — I had little choice but to hold back. Over the years, I began to understand the difference between home ownership and stewardship — and the realization that what we choose to remove is rarely the end of the story. Fast-forward to 2026 and the numbers around construction waste are staggering. Home renovations contribute roughly six pounds of waste per square foot , according to waste management and site services company Prime Dumpster Inc. For a modest 1,500-square-foot kitchen and bathroom remodel, that’s nearly 9,000 pounds of materials heading, well, somewhere. And that’s just the building materials. Consider the furniture: According to the EPA , Americans send over 12 million tons of furniture to landfills every year (that’s enough to fully furnish nearly nine million people in one-bedroom apartments). Most of that furniture is less than 15 years old , and about 80% of discarded furniture ends up buried rather than reused. The good news? That “somewhere” doesn’t have to be a landfill. In and around Marin, we have great resources that make sustainable renovation not just possible, but surprisingly straightforward. It takes a little planning upfront, and the rewards can be more than just feeling good. Why Sustainable Renovation Matters Beyond the sheer volume of waste, there’s a compelling environmental case for thoughtful disposal. According to a 2021 report published by World Green Building Council , the global construction industry consumes about 50% of all raw materials used worldwide. Every cabinet, fixture and flooring plank that gets salvaged rather than trashed reduces the demand for virgin resources and the energy required to manufacture replacements. There’s also California laws to consider: CALGreen requirements mandate that projects of 5,000 square feet or more must divert at least 65% of construction and demolition debris from landfills through recycling or reuse. Even if your project is smaller, these guidelines offer a useful framework for responsible renovation. Finally, if questions around cost come to mind, you’ll be pleasantly surprised to find out this doesn’t have to be expensive. While it may take more planning, donation receipts can help offset costs, recycling can reduce landfill tipping fees, and some materials — particularly metals like copper and brass — have resale value. Deconstruction Over Demolishing Courtesy of Stocksy Traditional demolition prioritizes speed: bring in the sledgehammers and dumpsters, clear everything out as fast as possible. But deconstruction takes a different approach, carefully dismantling valuable building components to preserve them for reuse. Beyond doing the “right thing” for your community and the environment, certain financial benefits may be gained by deconstructing your house and donating the salvaged building material, including tax deductions. Most deconstruction contractors, like Rebuild Green and MPH Deconstruction , will handle the whole process for you, from planning and permitting to site cleanup. Furniture and Household Items Before renovation begins, you’ll likely need to clear rooms of furniture and household goods. Your sofa, dining set and bedroom furniture, however, don’t have to end up in landfills. In fact, several Marin organizations, like Make It Home and Welcoming Home , accept donations of items in good condition, turning your castoffs into meaningful support for neighbors in need. Consignment stores, such as Design Plus Consignment Gallery , Home Consignment Center or Peterson’s Consign & Design are another great option, or you can simply post your item(s) for sale or for free on online marketplaces. Building Materials and Fixtures Cabinets, doors, windows, lighting fixtures and plumbing hardware in good condition often have significant reuse value. Most of the building materials collection and reuse facilities are in surrounding counties, like Heritage Salvage of Petaluma , and Building Resources of San Francisco . Before you plan a drop-off, always contact facilities before drop-off to confirm they’ll accept your specific items and learn about any condition standards. Construction Debris Courtesy of iStock For materials that can’t be reused as-is (think broken concrete, mixed lumber, drywall), the Marin Resource Recovery Center is your destination. Part of the Marin Sanitary Service family, this facility processes nearly 3,000 tons of recyclables monthly using a sophisticated system of screens, conveyors, magnets and hand-sorting to separate and recover materials. Hazardous Materials Hazardous materials should never be placed in regular trash or recycling — they can contaminate entire loads and pose risks to workers and the environment.If you need help with removal, the Marin Household Hazardous Waste Facility accepts paint, solvents, pesticides and other hazardous materials requiring special handling at no charge. Making Sustainable Choices Easy Zero Waste Marin , a program of Marin County Public Works, offers comprehensive resources for construction and demolition projects. Their website provides a directory of certified facilities that specialize in reuse and deconstruction, along with downloadable C&D Recycling Plan forms to help you track your diversion efforts. If managing disposal feels overwhelming, services like Hauling 911 specialize in eco-friendly debris removal for renovations. Remoov in San Francisco will pick up unwanted appliances and furniture (you can call for a free estimate). If, however, your project is extensive or prolonged, consider hiring a professional home organizer to handle everything, like Next Highest Good or Sort Marin . They have the skills and resources to sort, store, sell, haul, auction, donate and move out an entire household. (Well, maybe not your pets and children). The Bigger Picture Courtesy of Stocksy A home renovation is inherently an act of optimism — an investment in making your living space better suited to your life. Approaching the process through a thoughtful, sustainability lens extends that optimism outward, ensuring that your improvement doesn’t come at the expense of our shared environment. I was eventually able to do an extensive remodel of the kitchen and adjoining rooms in our cottage. Time and lived experience had taught me to appreciate the story of the home, and I wanted to preserve its character and honor the craftsmanship, if not all the fixtures, while doing so responsibly. What had the most impact was deciding what not to do. Rather than push the exterior walls out and expand the home’s footprint, I chose to remove interior walls and vault the ceiling instead. That decision allowed me to invest in high-quality, long-lasting appliances. I was also able to maintain many of the original light fixtures, which were also refurbished to extraordinary results. As for the waste, my contractor carefully sorted, salvaged and recycled much of our debris, rather than sending it all to the landfill. As I found out, first hand, the infrastructure to renovate responsibly exists. And, with a little planning, your renovation can demonstrate that beautiful, functional homes and environmental responsibility go hand in hand. Your discarded cabinets could help build someone’s first home. The Hidden Tradeoffs of Living in a Protected Place Marin County protects more open space per capita than almost anywhere in California, with roughly 40–50% of its land preserved or restricted. That commitment to conservation is one of the reasons the county is so beautiful — but it also comes with trade-offs. Salvage yards, reuse centers, and material recovery facilities need large, affordable, industrial spaces and Marin simply doesn’t have much of that. With strict land-use rules and a community that values beauty, order, and a strong sense of place, many of the systems that handle our waste and reuse — salvage, recycling and processing — are located in neighboring counties. That’s why deconstruction and hauling materials to reuse facilities can feel inconvenient and cost more. The tradeoff, of course, is living in one of the most beautiful and protected counties in the state. Kathryn Soter serves as Executive Director of the Good Future Design Alliance (GFDA) , and is on the SPACES Advisory Board. She spearheads GFDA programming and educational initiatives in low-waste and circular design, and regularly moderates panels at high-profile industry events. The post A Place for Everything: Your Home Renovation Disposal Guide appeared first on Marin Magazine .

title: DirtySpoon with Love Handles
description: Dirtyspoon is arguably the most underrated, most important and most beloved band in this entire bio. Deftly (Deaf-ly?), navigating various genres , the Dirtyspoon bus ride makes stops at 80s Alt, Big Hair Spandex Metal,…
content: Dirtyspoon is arguably the most underrated, most important and most beloved band in this entire bio. Deftly (Deaf-ly?), navigating various genres , the Dirtyspoon bus ride makes stops at 80s Alt, Big Hair Spandex Metal, Nostalgia Pop and Familiar Funk. We believe that the key to a great party is getting the ladies on the dance floor. And you know we're right fellas. Dirtyspoon recently underwent genetic testing and it turns out our ancestry includes Ska, Reggae, Country, Nu Metal, Hip Hop, Emo, ShoeGaze, Pop and Blues. And Icelandic. Dirtyspoon covers highly danceable hits from the 80s, 90s but You Aughta Know that already. We have been favorably compared to, in no particular order, the Joe Montana era 49ers, a bottle of 1982 Mouton Rothschild and the second season of Hill Street Blues. Equally comfortable covering Come on Eileen… Sweet Child of mine… And Uptown Funk … Dirtyspoon is the soundtrack to your misspent youth …missed opportunities …and the one who got away.

title: Boys lacrosse falls to De La Salle in early-season test
description: Boys varsity lacrosse dropped a 15-4 loss to De La Salle Thursday, but the shorthanded Giants say the loss carries lessons they plan to bring into the postseason. De La Salle opened strong, scoring four straight goals b…
content: Boys varsity lacrosse dropped a 15-4 loss to De La Salle Thursday, but the shorthanded Giants say the loss carries lessons they plan to bring into the postseason. De La Salle opened strong, scoring four straight goals before the Giants brought the gap to 4–5 by the end of the second quarter. Despite the Giants gaining confidence, after halftime the momentum shifted back to De La Salle, who pulled away with eight unanswered goals to seal a dominant 15–4 final. Sophomore Joe Connolly said the team came in with a clear game plan. Sophomore Joe Connolly charges onto the field to start the second half. ( Solomon Strasburg ) “Our game plan was coming in strong, keeping the momentum on the offensive side,” Connolly said. But missing key players made execution difficult, and De La Salle’s depth became a deciding factor early on. Sophomore Knox Donnelly described the moment things turned. “After we went on a run to make it 5-4, they went on a big run, and our energy just plateaued after that and dropped off,” Donelly said. Both players said De La Salle emphasized that depth was a key factor in the game. With a bigger roster, De La Salle’s players stayed fresh while Redwood’s wore down. “They had a lot more bodies than we did, and they were a lot fresher than we were,” Donnelly said. Adjustments were made mid-game, but the deficit proved too large to close. Connolly said the biggest takeaway from the game was learning the team needs to improve their physical conditioning. “We need to keep our energy up throughout the whole game. Mentally, we were good, but physically, we need to stay strong and make sure we make every possession count instead of turning over those transitions,” Connoly said. Spencer Springfield darts past defenders en route to a goal. ( Solomon Strasburg ) Donnelly kept it simple about what needs to change “The whole team needs to work harder, get in better shape and practice the fundamentals,” Donnelly said. Despite the loss, both players said they aren’t worried. A Marin County Athletic League (MCAL) title and a shot at North Coast Sectionals (NCS) are still on the table in May, and they see early losses as part of getting ready for what matters most. “These early games are supposed to prepare us for May,” Connolly said, “When it really matters.”
